author: bharath.vedartham
content:
i dont think we can tell from the bid that a builder has satisfied the IL without some sort of merkle proofs on the transaction root(which will need extending the bid structure and also increase its size with the proofs) 
I think I would like to question more why the bits needs to exist as the biggest defence against not satisfying the IL would be that the next proposer just simply doesn't extend on that block which is generally bad for the builder.

author: nflaig
content: it's part of devnet-5 per [doc](https://notes.ethereum.org/@ethpandaops/glamsterdam-devnet-5), and latest dora image already uses those

author: jih2nn
content:
As <@1035747431461167125> pointed out, the original intention is to signal a proposer that the corresponding payload would be IL-compliant. When a builder found out lied about their bidlist, validators can disconnect from the builder.

I'm personally not aware of other use case than this. At the same time, it doesn't add that much complexity to the protocol. I've been fifty-fifty regarding the IL bitlist.

author: jih2nn
content:
After discussing with <@586161934425128960>, I think there are three options.

1. Change the engine API to send a list of ILs, and let the EL to do deduplication. An IL at each index corresponds to the IL committee member at the index. `NULL` represents a missing IL. The deduplication becomes more efficient as the EL is aware of RLP—previously the CL did byte-to-byte comparisons—but now the engine API passes duplicate transactions.

2. Remove the bitlist. This is about whether we think the bitlist is worth to have. FYI, people agreed that it's worth to have that were in the FOCIL breakout in Svalbard (thanks to the use case mentioned above). Keen to learn if this has changed as more people chime in.

3. Leave everything else as-is and modify `payload_attributes` event to include `inclusion_list_bits`. `payload_attributes` event has matched the actual payloadAttributes parameter, so this will be an exception from that perspective.

Would love to learn what people prefer.

author: jih2nn
content:
This is a good point. A payload can be IL-compliant even when it didn't consider some IL when the omitted IL doesn't add any new transaction. This is why I didn't consider an IL bitlist in the forkchoice. Because what matters is whether a payload satisfies the IL, rather than whether the bitlist is a superset to your IL view.

The bitlist is a protection for a proposer that signals the corresponding payload will be IL-compliant. But a counterargument is that all payloads are meant to be IL-compliant anyway.

You can also disconnect from a builder who produced a non-IL-compliant payload, which is possible even without the bitlist.

author: bharath.vedartham
content: I am fine with (1) and (3) from buildoor and builder perspective. as long as there is some way to derive the inclusion_list_bits

author: bharath.vedartham
content: yeah disconnecting from a builder will be interesting with ePBS. You can't disconnect from a p2p builder but you can disconnect from a builder via the builder-api

author: bharath.vedartham
content: we have no way to slash a builder outside of the fact that, even if the payload's IL is not satisfied, the builders trustless payment will go thru

author: bharath.vedartham
content: I dont see any gossip rules telling the proposer to ignore or reject the bid if it is not a superset of their inclusion list store? if we have the bits, should we add that?

author: jih2nn
content: Because it's the proposer who decides that, not validators who gossiping bids.
